title: Clank & Slop — The Tape
tagline: — READ THE TAPE · ALL THE SLOP THAT'S FIT TO PRICE —

props.label: At a Glance · From the Tape Desk

bullets.text: Hormuz is moving again, which is not the same as normal. CENTCOM counted 55 merchant ships carrying more than 17 million barrels in a single day, two stranded VLCCs are exiting and seven QatarEnergy-controlled empty LNG tankers ballasted back into the Gulf between June 11 and 22 — the first Qatar-linked return since February. Against a pre-war denominator of 125–140 daily passages, a strong print proves reopening pressure, not restored routine.

Oil priced the opening, not the tollbooth. Brent settled Monday down 3.31% to $77.90 after the US-Iran talks, the 60-day waiver and early shipping resumption cut the disruption premium, then sat near $77.64 Tuesday. The Treasury license permits Iranian crude and petrochemical sales through August 21, but PVM's Tamas Varga warned shipowners still need assurance the mine threat is gone before this reads as an unconditional return.

The gate is becoming financial, not only nautical. Iran's PGSA waives planned fees during the 60-day window but requires transit requests filed at least 48 hours ahead, passage only through the designated route near Larak Island, and mandatory PGSA-approved insurance — free for now, with Iran reserving the right to introduce fees later. Washington has called a tolling system something it "will not tolerate," so the compliance question survives the reopening.

Qatar raised the Ras Laffan toll to 13 killed and 66 injured after missing workers were declared dead. The Barzan blast struck a domestic-gas facility — 1.4 BSCFD of sales gas for local power, desalination and industry — not one of the 14 LNG export trains. Ras Laffan moves about 20% of global LNG across 77 mtpa, so location gives the incident relevance, but with no evidence of sabotage the casualty and export-arithmetic stories stay separate.

props.title: Hormuz: a strong print, not a routine
props.subtitle: Strait transit reality · vessels per day
props.source: Reuters (~125–140/day pre-war commercial norm and 20% of world energy supplies), CENTCOM (55 merchant ships, June 20)

items.what: Hormuz: the permit/fee regime and insured tonnage
items.why: Normalization needs sustained two-way transits filed through PGSA, mandatory Iranian-approved insurance that stays free, and no enforced fee during the 60-day window; the durable shift is that passage is now an administered privilege — permits, one route near Larak, fees reserved for later — not a closed gate.

items.what: Lake Lucerne implementation · the Lebanon de-confliction cell
items.why: Washington moved first through sanctions licensing while Tehran disputed the IAEA-inspection premise; the cell brings in the US, Iran, Lebanon, Qatar and Pakistan but excludes Israel, which holds full freedom of action, and Israeli gunfire killed two near Nabatieh on Tuesday as a fifth round of direct talks opened in Washington.

The Security Council has demanded the RSF halt an imminent offensive on a city the HRC says puts ~500,000 civilians at risk; ten consecutive days of drone strikes and encirclement are running without verified ground entry, so the prevention window is open precisely because the full assault is not yet confirmed.

items.what: Autonomy-war buildout · counterspace RPO and strike-drone warheads
items.why: VICTUS HAZE joined responsive launch to orbital rendezvous-and-proximity operations while Helsing's EURENCO MoU pushes European autonomy toward sovereign warhead energetics; the frontier is moving from demonstrators toward platforms, reference architectures and the explosive chain around them.

No independent evaluation of Sakana's Fugu Ultra has appeared and Anthropic has not named the Qwable distill; access controls slow direct use but the open question is whether orchestration and disputed traces let restricted capability be approximated or leaked faster than it can be verified.

items.what: India said four fertilizer cargo ships crossed Hormuz toward Indian ports last week after reporting 16 India-bound fertilizer ships stranded — a directional reopening across energy-adjacent bulk flows, while the larger queue and daily-passage denominator stay unresolved.

Lloyd's List describes the mechanism underneath Iran's claim: mandatory PGSA-approved insurance, initially provided free of charge, with Iran reserving the right to introduce insurance fees in the future — a hull cleared to transit can still face administrative cost the moment the window closes.

Iranian strikes in March had already removed 17% of LNG export capacity — 12.8 mtpa across two trains and a GTL unit — for three to five years; the Barzan blast adds a casualty story but, on the public record, no confirmed disruption to export loadings.
